title: 2  ggplot basics
text:
2.0.1 Introduction
Sources:
ggplot2 is a system for creating graphics, based on The Grammar of Graphics. You provide data, tell ggplot2 how to map variables to aesthetics, what graphical primitives to use, and it takes care of the details.

title: 2  ggplot basics
text:
2.1 The structure of statistical graphics
A mapping of data variables to aesthetic attributes of geometric objects.
All ggplot2 graphs have these three elements:

a data set (data),
a geometry (geom),
an aesthetic mapping (aes).

The data element is intuitive to me. It is whatever data you feed into the command, where to look for the variables.
What is a geometric object? In the simple base R graph above, it is a point. It could be a line, a polygon…
What are the aesthetic attributes of a point? In the base R graph, we have an x co-ordinate, and a y co-ordinate. To construct a plot, we tell the software where to find those two co-ordinates, and therefore where to locate the point. So the aesthetic attributes tell you how to go from the dataset to identify the aesthetic qualities of the graph.
James Scott describes these features as adding layers to a plot, each one is a layer, linked by a + symbol.


2.1.1 The grammar of graphics



The grammar of graphics





2.1.2 An example
This creates a ggplot. The first ‘layer’ tells ggplot to look at the mpg data, the second layer makes an “aesthetic mapping” (aes) by:

linking the variable displ with the aesthetic property x (horizontal location),
and linking the variable hwy with the aesthetic property y (vertical location).

It then displays the data in a scatter plot, the chosen geometry (geom_point).
Some people think this graph looks nicer than base R’s graph, but there is more to it than that.

ggplot(data=mpg) + 
  geom_point(mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy))

title: 2  ggplot basics
text:
2.2 Adding layers to a graph
ggplot2 allows more aesthetic mappings to be added, with more than 2 variables.

2.2.1 Point colour
Here the colour option is varied by the class of vehicle.

ggplot(data=mpg) +
  geom_point(mapping=aes(x=displ, y=hwy, colour=class))









We can also vary colour using a numeric variable, where it produces a fade effect.

ggplot(data=mpg) +
  geom_point(mapping=aes(x=displ, y=hwy, colour=displ))











2.2.2 Fill
Fill performs a similar function to colour.
However, with geom_point to be able to use fill we need to select a point ‘shape’ that allows it.
For geom_point, one usable shape 21 (possible ones are 21-25).
When shape = 21 there is a space inside the point to be filled. Without it, the fill argument has no effect.

ggarrange(

  ggplot(data=mpg) +
  geom_point(mapping=aes(x=displ, y=hwy, colour = drv),  fill="white", shape=21, size=3.5), 
  
  ggplot(data=mpg) +
  geom_point(mapping=aes(x=displ, y=hwy, colour = drv),  fill="white")
  
  )









With other geoms fill works automatically, as with geom_histogram below.

ggplot(data=mpg) +
  geom_histogram(mapping=aes(y=hwy, fill = drv)) + 
  coord_flip()

`stat_bin()` using `bins = 30`. Pick better value `binwidth`.












2.2.3 Point size
You can also edit other properties of graphs, for example, point size. Although that makes limited sense in this case.
R gives a warning here, because categorical variables are not best reflected by point size.

ggplot(data=mpg) +
  geom_point(mapping=aes(x=displ, y=hwy, size=class))
## Warning: Using size for a discrete variable is not advised.









Instead, a numeric variable would make more sense.

ggplot(data = mpg) + 
  geom_point(mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy, size = displ, colour=displ))











2.2.4 Point transparency
We could also vary the point by transparency:

ggplot(data=mpg) +
  geom_point(mapping=aes(x=displ, y=hwy, alpha=class))
## Warning: Using alpha for a discrete variable is not advised.










2.2.5 Point shape
You can also vary point shape. ()

ggplot(data=mpg) +
  geom_point(mapping=aes(x=displ, y=hwy, shape=class))
## Warning: The shape palette can deal with a maximum of 6 discrete values because more
## than 6 becomes difficult to discriminate
## ℹ you have requested 7 values. Consider specifying shapes manually if you need
##   that many of them.
## Warning: Removed 62 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
## (`geom_point()`).











2.2.6 Shape outline
Stroke controls the width of the border of a shape. It’s only suitable for shapes which have a border.
Shape is set to 21 so that the border can be distinguished from the fill colour.


ggplot(data=mpg) +
  geom_point(mapping=aes(x=displ, y=hwy, stroke=2), shape = 21)








When applied to a line chart we get the warning below, indicating stroke is ignored.


ggplot(data=mpg) +
  geom_smooth(mapping=aes(x=displ, y=hwy, stroke=2))
## Warning in geom_smooth(mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy, stroke = 2)): Ignoring
## unknown aesthetics: stroke
## `geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x'











2.2.7 Combining layers
Multiple characteristics can also be applied in the same plot. Here the displ variable controls both the size and colour of the points (also shown, but not explained, above).

ggplot(data = mpg) + 
  geom_point(mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy, size = displ, colour=displ))









You can get quite nuanced with this. In the code below:

colour sets the border of the shape to white.

colour is visible only because shape equals 21, with other values of shape it would not be visible.

size controls point size.

fill, with aes(), fills the points with colours linked to the drv variable, again visible only because shape = 21.


ggplot(data = mpg, mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy)) + 
  geom_point(mapping = aes(fill = drv), shape=21, colour="white", size=3, stroke=2)











2.2.8 Transforming numeric to logical variables
Here ‘displ < 5’ is a logical - TRUE/FALSE - statement. The TRUE cases are one colour, the FALSE are another.

ggplot(data = mpg) + 
geom_point(mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy, colour = displ < 5))











2.2.9 Adding data labels
Rather surprisingly font size has a dramatic effect. Size 11 is huge.
Also an issue around how to intelligently extend the plot margins to contain the data labels when outside the bars. How far they should extend depends on the plot size, which differs depending on faceting and similar.

chart <- mpg %>% 
  group_by(drv) %>%
  summarise(hwy = mean(hwy, na.rm = TRUE)) %>% 
  ggplot(aes(x = drv, y = hwy)) + 
  geom_col() 

chart







chart2 <- chart + geom_text(aes(label = hwy))

chart3 <- chart + geom_text(aes(label = round(hwy), vjust = -1)) + 
  coord_cartesian(ylim = c(0, 35))

chart4 <- chart + geom_text(aes(label = round(hwy,1)), vjust = 2, size = 11, color = "white", size.unit = "pt") + 
  theme(legend.position = "none")

ggarrange(chart, chart2, chart3, chart4, ncol = 2, nrow = 2)









Note the font size of bars cannot be set within theme using text = element_text (more on this below).
One tricky thing is that this size is in mm while the size of element_text() is in pt.
If you want to match geom_text()’s font size to the theme’s size, specify size.unit = “pt” in geom_text()

ggarrange(
  
chart + geom_text(aes(label = round(hwy)), vjust = -1, size = 11) + 
  coord_cartesian(ylim = c(0, 35)) + 
  theme(text = element_text(size = 11)),

chart + geom_text(aes(label = round(hwy)), vjust = -1, size = 11, size.unit = "pt") + 
  coord_cartesian(ylim = c(0, 35)) + 
  theme(text = element_text(size = 11)),

nrow = 2)

title: 2  ggplot basics
text:
2.3 Adding facets
Stratifying a plot is done with the facet_wrap commands, it runs a separate graph for each sub-group.
The facet layer is considered a ‘third’ layer to the graph. The geometry and aesthetic mapping above are actually part of one layer.

ggplot(data=mpg) + 
  geom_point(mapping=aes(x=displ, y=hwy)) +
  facet_wrap(~ class, nrow=2)







 # The nrow specifies display results over two rows. 

Switching to control number of columns…

ggplot(data=mpg) + 
  geom_point(mapping=aes(x=displ, y=hwy)) +
  facet_wrap(~ class, ncol=2)








If the number of row and columns is specified and does not allow enough panels, you will get an error.

ggplot(data=mpg) + 
  geom_point(mapping=aes(x=displ, y=hwy)) +
  facet_wrap(~ class, ncol=2, nrow=2)
## Error in `wrap_dims()`:
## ! Need 7 panels, but together `nrow` and `ncol` only provide 4.
## ℹ Please increase `ncol` and/or `nrow`.



2.3.0.1 Using a continuous variable as a facet argument
Does not work so well.

ggplot(data = mpg) + 
  geom_point(mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy)) + 
  facet_wrap(~ displ)











2.3.1 Using a grid display
You can also divide your data by a combination of two variables, but frankly this is confusing.

ggplot(data = mpg) + 
  geom_point(mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy)) + 
  facet_grid(drv ~ cyl)










2.3.1.1 Switch to a horizontal split for facet_grid
As standard, grids are split along one row with multiple columns.

ggplot(data = mpg) + 
  geom_point(mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy)) + 
  facet_grid(~drv)








Add ~. to present this with one column and multiple rows.

ggplot(data = mpg) + 
  geom_point(mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy)) + 
  facet_grid(drv ~ .)












2.3.2 Adding further layers
Additional layers can be added after this.
Here, labels.

ggplot(data=mpg) + 
  geom_point(mapping=aes(x=displ, y=hwy)) +
  facet_wrap(~ class, nrow=2) + 
  labs(
    title="Fuel efficiency generally decreases with engine size",
    caption="Data from fueleconomy.gov",
    x="Engine displacement (litres)",
    y="Highway gas mileage (mpg)"
  )

title: 2  ggplot basics
text:
2.4 Saving plots as an object
This saves your basic plot as an object, and you can now experiment with adding more layers to it, and see what you like best.

pl <- ggplot(data=mpg) + 
  geom_point(mapping=aes(x=displ, y=hwy))


Here we try adding a facet_wrap using class, with one row of panels.

pl + facet_wrap(~class, nrow=1)








If we don’t like that, try it with two.

pl + facet_wrap(~class, nrow=2)

title: 2  ggplot basics
text:
2.5 Manually changing plot characteristics
You can also manually change an aesthetic property by putting it outside the aes() command.
Here we simply make all the points blue.

ggplot(data=mpg) + 
  geom_point(mapping = aes(x=displ, y=hwy), colour="blue")









Here colour needs to be specified outside of the aes() argument, otherwise it has no effect. There is no error warning helpfully.

ggplot(data = mpg) + 
  geom_point(mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy, color = "blue"))









Or we can choose the shapes…

ggplot(data=mpg) +
  geom_point(mapping = aes(x=displ, y=hwy), shape=8)

title: 2  ggplot basics
section: 2.6 Using aes() on its own layer
text:
2.6 Using aes() on its own layer
The aes() step can form its own layer, separate to the geom_point() one.
With only the aes() step specified we have associated the x axis with displ and the y axis with hwy, but no graph is shown. geom_point() specifies the graph to be shown.

ggplot(data=mpg) + aes(x=displ, y=hwy)








Adding the graph type:

ggplot(data=mpg) + aes(x=displ, y=hwy) + geom_point()








This seems largely useless, but it does illustrate the differences between these steps, and possibly you could create an empty plot, with axis specified, and then feed that into different geom_point() steps. Not sure that’s really useful though.

title: 2  ggplot basics
text:
2.7 What is a geom?

To answer this, look at the two charts below and consider - how are they similar?
Both plots contain the same x variable, the same y variable, and both describe the same data. But the plots are not identical. Each plot uses a different visual object to represent the data. In ggplot2 syntax, we say that they use different geoms.

A geom is the geometrical object that a plot uses to represent data.

People often describe plots by the type of geom that the plot uses. For example, bar charts use bar geoms, line charts use line geoms, boxplots use boxplot geoms, and so on. Scatterplots break the trend; they use the point geom.
As we see below, you can use different geoms to plot the same data. The plot on the left uses the point geom, and the plot on the right uses the smooth geom, a smooth line fitted to the data.


ggarrange(
  ggplot(data = mpg) + geom_point(mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy)), 
  ggplot(data = mpg) + geom_smooth(mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy))
)
## `geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x'









Every geom function in ggplot2 takes a mapping argument. However, not every aesthetic works with every geom.
You could set the shape of a point, but you couldn’t set the “shape” of a line. On the other hand, you could set the linetype of a line. geom_smooth() will draw a different line, with a different linetype, for each unique value of the variable that you map to linetype.
Here geom_smooth() separates the cars into three lines based on their drv value, which describes a car’s drivetrain. One line describes all of the points with a 4 value, one line describes all of the points with an f value, and one line describes all of the points with an r value. Here, 4 stands for four-wheel drive, f for front-wheel drive, and r for rear-wheel drive.

ggplot(data = mpg) + 
  geom_smooth(mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy, linetype = drv))

`geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x'

title: 2  ggplot basics
section: 2.8 Putting multiple geoms in a plot
text:
2.8 Putting multiple geoms in a plot
If the above sounds strange, we can make it more clear by overlaying the lines on top of the raw data and then coloring everything according to drv.
To display multiple geoms in the same plot, add multiple geom functions to ggplot().
In this plot we pass the mapping function to ggplot at the same time as the data command. ggplot2 will treat these mappings as global mappings that apply to each geom in the graph.

ggplot(data = mpg, mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy, linetype = drv, colour=drv)) + 
  geom_smooth() + 
  geom_point()
## `geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x'









Alternatively, you can achieve the same by specifying the aesthetic arguments for each geom separately.


ggplot(data = mpg) + 
  geom_point(mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy, colour=drv)) +
  geom_smooth(mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy, linetype = drv, colour=drv))
## `geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x'









However, the code above introduces some duplication in our code.
Imagine if you wanted to change the y-axis to display cty instead of hwy. You’d need to change the variable in two places, and you might forget to update one. You can avoid this type of repetition by passing a set of mappings to ggplot() (as in the first chart above).
Whereas, if you place mappings in a geom function, ggplot2 will treat them as local mappings for the layer. It will use these mappings to extend or overwrite the global mappings for that layer only. This makes it possible to display different aesthetics in different layers.
Here colour is specified separately for the line and the scatter plot points.


ggplot(data = mpg, mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy)) + 
  geom_smooth(colour="black") + 
  geom_point(mapping=aes(colour=class))
## `geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x'

title: 2  ggplot basics
text:
2.9 Different data for different layers…
You can use the same idea to specify different data for each layer.
Here, our smooth line displays just a subset of the mpg dataset, the subcompact cars.
The local data argument in geom_smooth() overrides the global data argument in ggplot() for that layer only.
Notes:

The standard colours in ggplot can be found here, the lilac colur for subcompact cars has been manually selected for the line so it matches the point colours used for it.
The se=FALSE argument removes the confidence interval around the smooth line. Assuming that is a confidence interval.


ggplot(data = mpg, mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy)) + 
  geom_point(mapping = aes(color = class)) + 
  geom_smooth(data = filter(mpg, class == "subcompact"), colour="#C77CFF", se = FALSE)
## `geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x'

title: 2  ggplot basics
text:
2.10 The group aesthetic
Many geoms, like geom_smooth(), use a single geometric object to display multiple rows of data. For these geoms, you can set the group aesthetic to a categorical variable to draw multiple objects. ggplot2 will draw a separate object for each unique value of the grouping variable.
In practice, ggplot2 will automatically group the data for these geoms whenever you map an aesthetic to a discrete variable (as in the linetype example). It is convenient to rely on this feature because the group aesthetic by itself does not add a legend or distinguishing features to the geoms.
You can also add the show.legend = FALSE subcommand if needed to suppress the legend.

ggplot(data = mpg) +
  geom_smooth(mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy, group = drv))
## `geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x'










2.10.0.1 Plots with and without legend

ggarrange(
  ggplot(data = mpg) + geom_smooth(mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy, color = drv),
    show.legend = FALSE),
  ggplot(data = mpg) + geom_smooth(mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy, color = drv))
)

`geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x'
`geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x'

title: 2  ggplot basics
text:
2.11 Colour palettes
You can manually define colour palettes and feed them to the scale_colour_manual argument.

manual_colour_palette <- c("palevioletred4", "seagreen3", "royalblue1")

ggplot(data = mpg, mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy, colour = drv)) + 
  geom_point() + 
  scale_color_manual(values = manual_colour_palette)









Or use predefined palettes from various packages, such RColorBrewer.

library(RColorBrewer)
#https://r-graph-gallery.com/38-rcolorbrewers-palettes.html

ggplot(data = mpg, mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy, colour = drv)) + 
  geom_point() + 
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set2")

title: 2  ggplot basics
text:
2.12 Label wrapping
In the below the margin is a space left around the plot (as a proportion of the plot width) to ensure labels do not run edge to edge. You can adjust this margin based on your layout needs.
char_width: is an estimate of the average width of a character. Adjust this based on your specific font and size.
For “sans” at font size 11 try 0.08 inches, I have left it as 0.1 as below to be generous. Size 12 apparently would only be 0.083333 inches.

# Example taken from chat GPT.
library(stringr)

# Function to dynamically calculate wrap width based on plot dimensions
dynamic_str_wrap <- function(text, plot_width = 7, num_labels = 1, char_width = 0.1, margin = 0.1) {
  # plot_width: The width of the plot in inches
  # num_labels: The number of labels on the axis
  # char_width: Estimated average width of a character in inches
  # margin: The proportion of total plot width to be left as margin
  
  # Calculate the available width per label, accounting for margins
  max_label_width <- (plot_width * (1 - margin)) / num_labels
  
  # Calculate the maximum number of characters per line
  max_chars <- max_label_width / char_width
  
  # Wrap the text
  str_wrap(text, width = floor(max_chars))
}

# Sample data
df <- data.frame(
  x = c("A very long label that should be wrapped appropriately based on plot width",
        "Short label", "Yet another label that is quite long and requires wrapping", 
        "Another long label that might also need wrapping to fit properly"),
  y = c(2, 1, 4, 3)
)

df

                                                                           x y
1 A very long label that should be wrapped appropriately based on plot width 2
2                                                                Short label 1
3                 Yet another label that is quite long and requires wrapping 4
4           Another long label that might also need wrapping to fit properly 3

# Calculate the number of labels (x-axis categories)
num_labels <- nrow(df)

# Apply dynamic wrapping to labels
df$x_wrapped <- sapply(df$x, dynamic_str_wrap, plot_width = 7, num_labels = num_labels, char_width = 0.1, margin = 0.1)

df

                                                                           x y
1 A very long label that should be wrapped appropriately based on plot width 2
2                                                                Short label 1
3                 Yet another label that is quite long and requires wrapping 4
4           Another long label that might also need wrapping to fit properly 3
                                                                         x_wrapped
1 A very long\nlabel that\nshould be\nwrapped\nappropriately\nbased on plot\nwidth
2                                                                      Short label
3                   Yet another\nlabel that\nis quite long\nand requires\nwrapping
4             Another long\nlabel that\nmight also need\nwrapping to fit\nproperly

# Plot with wrapped x-axis labels (kept horizontal)
ggplot(df, aes(x = x_wrapped, y = y)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity") +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 0, hjust = 0.5, vjust = 0.5, size = 11))








Adjusting the code above to work within a mutate framework.

df %>%
  mutate(x_wrapped = map_chr(x, dynamic_str_wrap, plot_width = 7, num_labels = num_labels, char_width = 0.08, margin = 0.1)) %>% 
  ggplot(aes(x = x_wrapped, y = y)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity") +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 0, hjust = 0.5, vjust = 0.5, size = 11))










2.12.1 If we have a factor variable we can amend to:

# Add factor to data frame
df <- data.frame(
  x = factor(c("A very long label that should be wrapped appropriately based on plot width",
        "Short label", "Yet another label that is quite long and requires wrapping", 
        "Another long label that might also need wrapping to fit properly")),
  y = c(2, 1, 4, 3)
)

df <- df %>%
  mutate(x_wrapped = map_chr(as.character(x), dynamic_str_wrap, plot_width = 7, num_labels = num_labels, char_width = 0.08, margin = 0.1))

# Unique does return the items in order.... 
# Even when convert to character as with x_wrapped
# Hence below it enforces current order on new variable. 
unique(df$x)

[1] A very long label that should be wrapped appropriately based on plot width
[2] Short label                                                               
[3] Yet another label that is quite long and requires wrapping                
[4] Another long label that might also need wrapping to fit properly          
4 Levels: A very long label that should be wrapped appropriately based on plot width ...

unique(df$x_wrapped)

[1] "A very long\nlabel that\nshould be wrapped\nappropriately based\non plot width"
[2] "Short label"                                                                   
[3] "Yet another label\nthat is quite\nlong and requires\nwrapping"                 
[4] "Another long label\nthat might also\nneed wrapping to\nfit properly"           

# Convert wrapped labels back to factors if needed
df <- df %>%
  mutate(x_wrapped = factor(x_wrapped, levels = unique(x_wrapped)))

# Plot with wrapped x-axis labels (kept horizontal)
ggplot(df, aes(x = x_wrapped, y = y)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity") +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 0, hjust = 0.5, vjust = 0.5, size = 11))

title: 2  ggplot basics
text:
2.13 element_text()
element_ _text() modifies label and headings.
You can control the font family, face, colour, size (in points), hjust, vjust, angle (in degrees) and lineheight (as ratio of fontcase). More details on the parameters can be found in vignette(“ggplot2-specs”).

text_amend <- 
  ggplot(data = mpg, mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy, colour = drv)) + 
  geom_point() + 
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set2") + 
  labs(title = "Highway miles per gallon by engine displacement") + 
  theme(plot.title = element_text(size = 12, face = "bold", colour = "red"))

# Edits also stack, or overwrite if previously specified
text_amend2 <- text_amend +  theme(plot.title = element_text(hjust = 1, colour = "black"))

ggarrange(text_amend, text_amend2, 
          nrow = 2)









You can also control the margins around text with margin(). The amount of sapce in points around the top, right, bottom and left sides of the text. Elements not specified equal zero.
There does not seem to be a uniform default for the margins.

ggarrange(text_amend + theme(plot.title = element_text(margin = margin())), 
          text_amend2 + theme(plot.title = element_text(margin = margin(t = 10, b = 10))), 
          nrow = 2)









With all axis headings specified.

text_amend <- 
  ggplot(data = mpg, mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy, colour = drv)) + 
  geom_point() + 
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set2") + 
  labs(title = "Highway miles per gallon by engine displacement", 
       x = "Engine displacement", 
       y = "Miles per gallon") + 
  theme(plot.title = element_text(size = 12, face = "bold"), 
        axis.title = element_text(size = 11, face = "bold"), 
        axis.title.y = element_text(vjust = 2), 
        axis.text = element_text(size = 11))

text_amend

title: 2  ggplot basics
text:
2.14 Theme elements
For this the modifying theme components section of the ggplot2 book.
There are around 40 unique elements that control the appearance of the plot. They can be roughly grouped into five categories: plot, axis, legend, panel and facet. The following sections describe each in turn.


2.14.1 Plot

plot.background - set by element_rect()

plot.title - set by element_text()

plot.margin - set by margin()

Of these I am quite happy with plot.background, and plot.title is covered above under text editing.
The plot.margin is by standard set to 5.5pt on all sides, for e.g. see theme_grey.

theme_grey()$plot.margin

NULL


Some example edits below, but perhaps 5.5 is ok too. It may be best to minimise to maximise plot space.

ggarrange(
text_amend + theme(plot.margin = margin(2, 2, 2, 2)), 
text_amend + theme(plot.margin = margin(10, 10, 10, 10)), 
nrow = 2)











2.14.2 Axis elements
There are several axis elements that can be controlled. Of these I am going to be most interested in:

axis.title, axis.title.x and axis.title.y - controlled by element_text().

Again I have covered these under text edits.
Maybe of interest is the angle of labels. The most common adjustment is to rotate the x-axis labels to avoid long overlapping labels. If you do this, note negative angles tend to look best and you should set hjust = 0 and vjust = 1:

df <- data.frame(
  x = c("label", "a long label", "an even longer label"), 
  y = 1:3
)
base <- ggplot(df, aes(x, y)) + geom_point()
base







base + 
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = -30, vjust = 1, hjust = 0)) + 
  xlab("axis label x") + 
  ylab("axis label y")








Note that margins set on axis text seem to apply only to axis.text, not axis.title. this may be a bug.

base + 
  theme(
  axis.title = element_text(margin = margin(t = 100)),
  axis.text.x = element_text(margin = margin(b = 10)), 
  axis.text.y = element_text(margin = margin(l = 10)))











2.14.3 Legends
The legend elements control the appearance of all legends. You can also modify the appearance of individual legends by modifying the same elements in guide_legend() or guide_colourbar().
This first set of example relate to legend size, background and text.

df <- data.frame(x = 1:4, y = 1:4, z = rep(c("a", "b"), each = 2))
base <- ggplot(df, aes(x, y, colour = z)) + geom_point()

ggarrange(
  
base + theme(
  legend.background = element_rect(
    fill = "lemonchiffon", 
    colour = "grey50", 
    linewidth = 1)),

base + theme(
  legend.key = element_rect(color = "grey50"),
  legend.key.width = unit(0.5, "cm"),
  legend.key.height = unit(0.75, "cm")),

base + theme(
  legend.text = element_text(size = 15),
  legend.title = element_text(size = 15, face = "bold"),
  legend.key.size = unit(0.25, "cm"),
  legend.margin = unit(0.1,  "cm")), # If set to zero removes margins entirely. 

ncol= 3)

Warning: `legend.margin` must be specified using `margin()`
ℹ For the old behavior use `legend.spacing`










This second set relate to legend position, direction, justification and the surrounding box.

plot <- ggplot(data = mpg, mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy, colour = drv)) + 
  geom_point() + 
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set2")

ggarrange(
plot + theme(legend.position = "left"),
plot + theme(legend.position = "right"), # the default 
plot + theme(legend.position = "bottom", legend.box.just = "left"),
plot + theme(legend.position = "none"),
ncol = 2, nrow = 2)











2.14.4 Panel elements
This is things like the panel grid, the panel border and the aspect ratio. By and large I will ignore these.



2.14.5 Facets
This can cotnrol the background of the panel strips,the strip text, and the panel spacing.
The default spacing between panels in faceted plot is 5.5pts.
Units: The spacing is set using the unit() function from the grid package. Common units include points (“pt”), millimeters (“mm”), and inches (“in”).

1 point (pt) is equal to 1/72 of an inch.

Therefore, 1 inch is equal to 72 points.


df <- data.frame(x = 1:4, y = 1:4, z = c("a", "a", "b", "b"))
base_f <- ggplot(df, aes(x, y)) + geom_point() + facet_wrap(~z)

base_f2 <- base_f + theme(panel.spacing = unit(0.5, "in"))

base_f3 <- base_f + theme(
  strip.text = element_text(colour = "white"),
  strip.background = element_rect(
    fill = "grey20", 
    color = "grey80", 
    linewidth = 1
  )
)

ggarrange(base_f, base_f2, base_f3, nrow = 1)

title: 3  ggplot colours
text:
3.1 Standard ggplot2 colours
The standard ggplot2 colours are pretty nice. To understand these see The default colours of ggplot2.
To view the basic colours in a chart…
library(tidyverse)
library(ggpubr)

#create data frame
df <- data.frame(team=c('A', 'B', 'C'),
                 points=c(22, 28, 15))

#create bar plot using df
ggplot(df, aes(x=team, y=points, fill=team)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity")
To obtain the hex codes of these colours we can use the hue_pal function…
library(scales)

#extract hex color codes for a plot with three elements in ggplot2 
hex <- hue_pal()(3)

#display hex color codes
hex
## [1] "#F8766D" "#00BA38" "#619CFF"

show_col(hex)
To see the colours ggplot2 will use as standard with different numbers of groups…
#set margins of plot area
par(mai = c(0.1, 0, 0.1, 0), bg = "grey85")

#create plot with ggplot2 default colors from 1 to 8
gc.grid <- layout(matrix(1:8, nrow = 8))
for(i in 1:8){
   gc.ramp <- hue_pal()(i)
   plot(c(0, 8), c(0,1),
        type = "n", 
        bty="n", 
        xaxt="n", 
        yaxt="n", xlab="", ylab="")
   for(j in 1:i){
      rect(j - 1, 0, j - 0.25, 1, col = gc.ramp[j])
   }
}
We can the also return the hex codes associated with them.
#display ggplot2 default hex color codes from 1 to 8
for(i in 1:8){
  print(hue_pal()(i))
}

title: 3  ggplot colours
text:
3.2 Other standard colour scales
See source here - the https://ggplot2-book.org/scales-colour.


3.2.1 Continuous scales
Colour gradients.. here illustrated with a 2d density estimate of the faithful dataset (Azzalini and Bowman 1990), which records the waiting time between eruptions and during each eruption for the Old Faithful geyser in Yellowstone Park.

eruptions numeric Eruption time in mins.

waiting numeric Waiting time to next eruption (in mins).

density 2d density estimate.

We hide the legends and set expand to 0, to focus on the appearance of the data.
Remember: although we use the erupt plot to illustrate concepts using with a fill aesthetic, the same ideas apply to colour scales. Any time we refer to scale_fill_() in this section there is a corresponding scale_colour_() for the colour aesthetic.

erupt <- ggplot(faithfuld, aes(waiting, eruptions, fill = density)) +
  geom_raster() +
  scale_x_continuous(NULL, expand = c(0, 0)) + 
  scale_y_continuous(NULL, expand = c(0, 0)) + 
  theme(legend.position = "none")

erupt







# scale_fill_gradient - produces a two-colour gradient
eruptb <- erupt + scale_fill_gradient(low = "#ff8200", high = "#7082d4")

# scale_fill_gradient2() produces a three-colour gradient with specified midpoint
eruptc <- erupt + scale_fill_gradient2(low = "#f25c91", mid = "white", high = "#00ab85", midpoint = mean(faithfuld$density))

# scale_fill_gradientn() produces an n-colour gradient
# Not sure of the use of this... 

ggarrange(erupt,
          eruptb,
          eruptc,
          labels = c("A", "B", "C"),
          ncol = 2, nrow = 2)









3.2.1.1 Missing data in continuous scales

df <- data.frame(x = 1, y = 1:5, z = c(1, 3, 2, NA, 5))
base <- ggplot(df, aes(x, y)) + 
  geom_tile(aes(fill = z), linewidth = 5) + 
  labs(x = NULL, y = NULL) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = NULL)

ggarrange(base,
          base + scale_fill_gradient(na.value = NA),
          base + scale_fill_gradient(na.value = "yellow"),
          labels = c("A", "B", "C"),
          ncol = 2, nrow = 2)











3.2.1.2 Limits, breaks, and labels

base <- ggplot(mpg, aes(cyl, displ, colour = hwy)) +
  geom_point(size = 2)

ggarrange(
  base,
  base + scale_color_gradient(limits = c(0, 100)),
  base + scale_color_gradient(breaks = c(10, 15, 20,25,30,35,40)),
  labels = c("A", "B", "C"),
  ncol = 2, nrow = 2)










3.2.1.3 Legends in continuous scales

basea <- base + guides(colour = guide_colourbar(reverse = TRUE))
baseb <- base + guides(colour = guide_colourbar(barheight = unit(2, "cm")))
basec <- base + guides(colour = guide_colourbar(direction = "horizontal"))

ggarrange(
  basea, baseb, basec,
  labels = c("A", "B", "C"),
  ncol = 2, nrow = 2)











3.2.1.4 What is a good colour scale for continous data?
My preference for most charts is one colour from opaque to low opacity. As shown below, when you run from one colour to another the results are often hard to follow. The exception perhaps are maps, where a third middle colour, that can be white, works quite well. This does not work for something like a dot plot.

base <- ggplot(mpg, aes(cyl, displ, colour = hwy)) +
  geom_point(size = 2) 

basea <- base + scale_color_gradient(low = "#ccede8",high = "#00ab85")
baseb <- base + scale_color_gradient(low = "#f25c91",high = "#7082d4")
basec <- base + scale_color_gradient(low = "#F8766D",high = "#619CFF")
based <- base + scale_color_gradient2(low = "#F8766D",high = "#00BFC4", mid = "white", 
                                     midpoint = median(mpg$hwy))

ggarrange(
  basea, baseb, basec,based,
  labels = c("A", "B", "C","D"),
  ncol = 2, nrow = 2)











3.2.1.5 Colour Brewer scales
Or Colour Brewer has a set of sequential scales to choose from.

RColorBrewer::display.brewer.all(type = "seq")









Of the below I like YlGn and OrRd.
Note use of scale_colour_distiller for distilling a categorised fill colour from a continuous variable.

basea <- base + scale_colour_distiller(palette = "Blues")
baseb <- base + scale_colour_distiller(palette = "YlGn")
basec <- base + scale_colour_distiller(palette = "OrRd")
based <- base + scale_colour_distiller(palette = "BuGn")

ggarrange(
  basea, baseb, basec,based,
  labels = c("A", "B", "C","D"),
  ncol = 2, nrow = 2)












3.2.2 Categorical colours

penguin <- palmerpenguins::penguins 

library(gtsummary)
penguin %>% tbl_summary(label = list(
  species ~ "Penguin species", 
  island ~ "Island in Palmer Archipelago", 
  bill_length_mm ~ "Bill length (millimeters)", 
  bill_depth_mm ~ "Bill depth (millimeters)",
  flipper_length_mm ~ "Flipper length (millimeters)",
  body_mass_g ~ "Body mass (grams)",
  sex ~ "Penguin sex",
  year ~ "Study year"), 
  missing_text = "Missing", 
  statistic = list(all_continuous() ~ "{mean}"))

For up to 3 colours I think basic ggplot does a great job.

species <- penguin %>% group_by(species) %>% summarise(n = n()) %>% 
  mutate(species_percent = 100 * (n/ sum(n)))

year <- penguin %>% group_by(year) %>% summarise(n = n()) %>% 
  mutate(year_percent = 100 * (n/ sum(n)))

sex <- penguin %>% group_by(sex) %>% summarise(n = n()) %>% 
  mutate(sex_percent = 100 * (n/ sum(n)))

island <- penguin %>% group_by(island) %>% summarise(n = n()) %>% 
  mutate(island_percent = 100 * (n/ sum(n)))

bar1 <- species %>% ggplot(aes(x = species, y = species_percent, fill = species)) + 
  geom_col()

bar2 <- sex %>% ggplot(aes(x = sex, y = sex_percent, fill = sex)) + 
  geom_col()

bar3 <- island %>% ggplot(aes(x = island, y = island_percent)) + 
  geom_col()

bar4 <- year %>% ggplot(aes(x = year, y = year_percent)) + 
  geom_col(fill = "#00BFC4")

ggarrange(
  bar1, bar2, bar3, bar4,
  labels = c("A", "B", "C", "D"),
  ncol = 2, nrow = 2)









Once you get beyond three, however, I think further colours are required.

penguin <- penguin %>% mutate(
  sex_island = paste0(island, ": ", sex)
)

penguin %>% count(sex_island)

# A tibble: 9 × 2
  sex_island            n

1 Biscoe: NA            5

bar5 <- penguin %>% count(sex_island) %>% 
  ggplot(aes(x = sex_island, y = n, fill = sex_island)) + 
  geom_col() + 
  coord_flip()

bar5









The ggplot standard above is actually not bad.
Alternatively, Colour Brewer provides a set of categorical data colour scales.
Note that there length differs in if there are not enough of them then those categories are missing.

RColorBrewer::display.brewer.all(type = "qual")









The ggplot2 book’s recommended options for areas are below, with the addition of Set1 which I like:

Pastel1

Pastel2

Set2

Accent

Whereas for points it suggests Set1 and Dark2.

library(RColorBrewer )
qual_palettes <- brewer.pal.info[brewer.pal.info$category == "qual", ]
print(qual_palettes)

Dark2           8     qual       TRUE
Paired         12     qual       TRUE

Set3           12     qual      FALSE

ggarrange(
  bar5 + scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set1"),
  bar5 + scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Pastel1"),
  bar5 + scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Pastel2"),
  bar5 + scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2"),
  bar5 + scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Accent"),
  labels = c("A", "B", "C", "D", "E"),
  ncol = 1, nrow = 5)

Returning the palette you asked for with that many colors

Returning the palette you asked for with that many colors










A last one from paletteer. Only has up to 5 colours. The Van Gogh palette. Not sure what I’d use it for.

bar1 + paletteer::scale_fill_paletteer_d("rtist::vangogh")









Alternatively with a NatCen colour palette.

The first option uses the second most opaque NatCen colours.

The second uses the most opaque and the fourth most opaque set of colours. * The third options is the first, third and least most opaque colours.

The final one is the most opaque and the least.


# For less than 5...
bar1 + scale_fill_manual(values = c("#8f99de","#f57da8","#33bd9e","#bf75b5","#ff9c33"))







# For UP TO 10...
bar5 + scale_fill_manual(values = c("#7082d4","#c7cced","#f25c91","#fabfd4","#00ab85","#99decf","#b053a1","#debad9","#ff8200","#ffcc99"))







# In an ideal world this might need to be (as the group variable has three levels): 
bar5 + 
  geom_col(color = "black") + 
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("#7082d4","#abb5e5","#e3e5f5",
                               "#f25c91","#f79ebf","#fcdee8",
                               "#00ab85","#66ccb5","#ccede8",
                               "#b053a1","#d199c7","#f0deed",
                               "#ff8200","#ffb566","#ffe5cc")) 







# Whereas with two levels it could look like this: 
penguin %>% 
  filter(!grepl(": NA", sex_island)) %>%
  count(sex_island) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = sex_island, y = n, fill = sex_island)) + 
  geom_col() + 
  coord_flip() + 
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("#7082d4","#c7cced","#f25c91","#fabfd4","#00ab85","#99decf","#b053a1","#debad9","#ff8200","#ffcc99"))










3.2.2.1 Fill colour for points points

# scatter plot
df <- data.frame(
  x = 1:3 + runif(30), 
  y = runif(30), 
  z = c("a", "b", "c")
)
point <- ggplot(df, aes(x, y)) +
  geom_point(aes(colour = z))  + 
  theme(legend.position = "none") +
  labs(x = NULL, y = NULL)

# three palettes
point # Basic one is quite nice here with limited set of data







point + scale_colour_brewer(palette = "Set1")







point + scale_colour_brewer(palette = "Dark2")

title: 3  ggplot colours
section: 3.3 A how to guide on defining palettes
text:
3.3 A how to guide on defining palettes
The below is taken from R for the rest of us.
Their first step is to define a list of colours as objects.

# Define Colors -----------------------------------------------------------

ga_purple <- "#8359AB"
ga_yellow <- "#FFDE39"
ga_gray <- "#827C78"
ga_blue <- "#49B8F1"
ga_brown <- "#B88262"
ga_pink <- "#DC458E"


They then put these in a function, so that these values are passed to the function scale_colour_manual each time it is run.
This first one is for categorical variables where the ordering of the colours is not meaningful.


# Qualitative -------------------------------------------------------------

scale_color_ga_qualitative <- function() {
  
  scale_color_manual(values = c(ga_blue, 
                                ga_pink, 
                                ga_yellow, 
                                ga_purple, 
                                ga_gray, 
                                ga_brown))
  
}

palmerpenguins::penguins %>%
  ggplot() +
  geom_point(aes(x = bill_length_mm, 
                 y = flipper_length_mm,
                 color = island)) +
  labs(title = "Palmer Penguins", 
       subtitle = "Look at them go!",
       x = "Bill length",
       y = "Flipper length") +
  theme_minimal() +
  scale_color_ga_qualitative()
## Warning: Removed 2 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
## (`geom_point()`).









Before the next example they load some interesting looking data about the 2020 US Presidential Election. They also load shapefiles from the tigris package for US Counties, so these results can be mapped onto a US electoral map.

# Download Data -----------------------------------------------------------

library(tigris)
## To enable caching of data, set `options(tigris_use_cache = TRUE)`
## in your R script or .Rprofile.

# Downloaded from https://github.com/tonmcg/US_County_Level_Election_Results_08-20
presidential_returns_by_county <- read_csv("https://github.com/tonmcg/US_County_Level_Election_Results_08-20/raw/master/2020_US_County_Level_Presidential_Results.csv")
## Rows: 3152 Columns: 10
## ── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
## Delimiter: ","
## chr (3): state_name, county_fips, county_name
## dbl (7): votes_gop, votes_dem, total_votes, diff, per_gop, per_dem, per_poin...
## 
## ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
## ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.


# Done with tigris package
us_counties <- counties()

Retrieving data for the year 2024




# Merge datasets
nc_presidential_returns_by_county <- us_counties %>% 
  left_join(presidential_returns_by_county,
            by = c("GEOID" = "county_fips")) %>% 
  filter(state_name == "North Carolina") %>% 
  mutate(county_name = str_remove(county_name, " County")) %>% 
  select(county_name, contains("votes"), per_point_diff) 


Next they do a sequential scale. Again, a function is defined where the progression of colour matters, setting this through the low and high arguments of the scale_fill_gradient function.

# Sequential --------------------------------------------------------------

scale_fill_ga_sequential <- function(low_color = ga_yellow, 
                                     high_color = ga_purple) {
  
  scale_fill_gradient(low = low_color, 
                      high = high_color)
  
}

nc_presidential_returns_by_county %>% 
  ggplot(aes(fill = total_votes)) +
  geom_sf() +
  theme_void() +
  scale_fill_ga_sequential()







nc_presidential_returns_by_county %>% 
  ggplot(aes(fill = total_votes)) +
  geom_sf() +
  theme_void() +
  scale_fill_ga_sequential(low_color = ga_purple,
                           high_color = ga_yellow)









To obtain diverging scale colours the scale_fill_gradient2 function allows a middle colour to be set.

# Diverging ---------------------------------------------------------------

scale_fill_ga_diverging <- function(low_color = ga_yellow, 
                                    medium_color = "white",
                                    high_color = ga_pink) {
  
  scale_fill_gradient2(low = low_color, 
                       mid = medium_color,
                       high = high_color)
  
}

nc_presidential_returns_by_county %>% 
  ggplot(aes(fill = per_point_diff)) +
  geom_sf() +
  theme_void() +
  scale_fill_ga_diverging()

4.1 Standard themes
The default theme, featuring a gray background and white gridlines.

4.1.1 theme_gray

plot <- ggplot(iris, aes(x = Sepal.Length, y = Sepal.Width, color = Species)) +
  geom_point()  

plot + theme_gray()











4.1.2 theme_bw
A black on white theme.

plot + theme_bw()











4.1.3 theme_linedraw
A theme with only black lines of various widths on white backgrounds.

plot + theme_linedraw()











4.1.4 theme_light
A theme similar to theme_linedraw but with grey lines and axes designed to draw more attention to the data.

plot + theme_light()











4.1.5 theme_dark
A theme similar to theme_light, but with a dark background. A useful theme for making thin colored lines stand out.

plot + theme_dark()











4.1.6 theme_minimal
A theme with no background annotations.

plot + theme_minimal()











4.1.7 theme_classic
A theme with no gridlines.

plot + theme_classic()











4.1.8 theme_void
A completely empty theme.

plot + theme_void()

title: 5  Bar charts
text:
5.1 Basics of bar charts
These uses the geom_col() function to display values in the data, geom_bar() to count the cases in a group.
In this example, average highway miles per gallon (hwy) is displayed by a the type of car (class).

(hwy_class <- mpg %>% 
  group_by(class) %>% 
  summarise_at(vars(hwy), list(average_hwy= ~round(mean(.x), digits = 1))))

# A tibble: 7 × 2
  class      average_hwy
  <chr>            <dbl>
1 2seater           24.8
2 compact           28.3
3 midsize           27.3
4 minivan           22.4
5 pickup            16.9
6 subcompact        28.1
7 suv               18.1




5.1.1 A basic bar chart
That data is then fed to ggplot to produce a simple bar chart.

ggplot(data=hwy_class) + 
  geom_col(mapping=aes(x=class, y=average_hwy))











5.1.2 A horizontal bar chart

ggplot(data=hwy_class) +
  geom_col(mapping=aes(x=class, y=average_hwy)) +
  coord_flip()

title: 5  Bar charts
text:
5.2 Amend fill colour


5.2.0.1 Standard gglot colours

plot <- ggplot(data=hwy_class) +
  aes(x=class, y=average_hwy, fill=class) +
  geom_col(width=0.5, colour = "black") +
  theme_minimal()

plot










5.2.0.2 Other options

ggarrange(
plot + scale_fill_grey() + theme(legend.position="none"),
plot + scale_fill_brewer(palette="PuRd") + theme(legend.position="none"),
plot + scale_fill_brewer(palette="Blues") + theme(legend.position="none"),
ncol = 1, nrow = 3)











5.2.1 Fill by another variable

(hwy_class_year <- mpg %>% 
  group_by(class, year) %>% 
  summarise_at(vars(hwy), list(average_hwy_year = ~round(mean(.x), 1))))

# A tibble: 14 × 3
# Groups:   class [7]
   class       year average_hwy_year
   <chr>      <int>            <dbl>
 1 2seater     1999             24.5
 2 2seater     2008             25  
 3 compact     1999             27.9
 4 compact     2008             28.7
 5 midsize     1999             26.5
 6 midsize     2008             28  
 7 minivan     1999             22.5
 8 minivan     2008             22.2
 9 pickup      1999             16.8
10 pickup      2008             16.9
11 subcompact  1999             29  
12 subcompact  2008             27.1
13 suv         1999             17.6
14 suv         2008             18.6



The second variable is now the fill colour.

ggplot(data=hwy_class_year, aes(x=class, y=average_hwy_year, fill=year)) +
  geom_col(width=0.5, colour="black")









The position argument (setting dodge2) moves the bars to appear alongside each other rather than stacked.

plot <- ggplot(data=hwy_class_year, aes(x=class, y=average_hwy_year, fill=year)) +
  geom_bar(stat="identity", position = "dodge2")

plot

title: 5  Bar charts
text:
5.3 Adding labels to data
The geom_text function is what adds the labels:

had to move the aes() bit out of the geom_bar() bit to make this work.

the vjust bit moves it above and below the bar. A lower minus number would be higher above the bar.


ggplot(data=hwy_class) +
  aes(x=class, y=average_hwy) +
  geom_bar(stat="identity") + 
  geom_text(aes(label=average_hwy), vjust=-1)









Here the label is below the bar, vjust=1.5. The colour has been switched to white and the size increased also.

ggplot(data=hwy_class) +
  aes(x=class, y=average_hwy) +
  geom_bar(stat="identity") + 
  geom_text(aes(label=average_hwy), vjust=1.5, colour="white",  size=6)









5.3.0.1 Adding them to our dodged plot above.
We also have to ‘dodge’ the position of the labels within geom_text, again in the position argument.

plot + 
  geom_text(aes(label=average_hwy_year), vjust=1.6, color="white",
            position = position_dodge2(0.9), size=3.5)

title: 5  Bar charts
text:
5.4 Other formatting


5.4.1 Bar width
Amend bar width within geom_col argument with ‘width’.
The default is 0.9, smaller values make bars smaller. Max = 1 and removes all space between bars.
If you make the entire graph wider or narrower, the bar dimensions will scale proportionally.

more_space <- ggplot(data=hwy_class) +
  geom_col(mapping=aes(x=class, y=average_hwy), width=0.9)

less_space <- ggplot(data=hwy_class) +
  geom_col(mapping=aes(x=class, y=average_hwy), width=0.5)

ggarrange(more_space, less_space, ncol = 1)











5.4.2 Change colours
In the below the colour affects only the outline, without the fill the bars would be dark grey still.

ggplot(data=hwy_class) +
  geom_col(mapping=aes(x=class, y=average_hwy), colour="blue", fill="white", width = 0.5)









Or bar colour can be changed by groups.

Add colour=var, where var is the variable you want to vary the colours by.


ggplot(data=hwy_class) +
  aes(x=class, y=average_hwy, colour=class) +
  geom_col(fill="white", width=0.5) 











5.4.3 Apply themes

p <- ggplot(data=hwy_class) +
  geom_col(mapping=aes(x=class, y=average_hwy), fill="steelblue") 

p + theme_minimal()

title: 5  Bar charts
text:
5.5 Select categories to show
You can also select which items to show:

ggplot(data=hwy_class) +
  geom_col(mapping=aes(x=class, y=average_hwy, label=average_hwy)) + 
  scale_x_discrete(limits=c("suv", "minivan", "pickup"))

Warning in geom_col(mapping = aes(x = class, y = average_hwy, label =
average_hwy)): Ignoring unknown aesthetics: label


Warning: Removed 4 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
(`geom_col()`).

title: 5  Bar charts
text:
5.6 Alternative statistics


5.6.0.1 Barplot of counts
Similar to a histogram, the stat=“bin” command allows ggplot() to work like table(), but specifically for numeric values.

ggplot(data=mpg) +
  aes(x=cyl) +
  geom_bar(stat="bin")

`stat_bin()` using `bins = 30`. Pick better value `binwidth`.










Or with character values, try geom_bar without an argument.

ggplot(data=mpg) +
  aes(x=class) +
  geom_bar()

5.7 Worked plots

library(haven)
library(labelled)
bsa <- read_sav("/Users/joecrowley/R/Data/BSA Teaching Data/spss/spss24/bsa2017_for_ukda.sav")
# names(bsa)



5.7.1 Bar chart for 1 way crossbreak (or for a frequency table)

bsa %>% count(as_factor(leftrigh, levels = "both"))

10 2.2                                      321
# ℹ 22 more rows

(leftrigh <- bsa %>% 
  mutate(dolefidl = as_factor(dolefidl), 
         leftrigh = case_when(leftrigh == 9 ~ NA, 
                              TRUE ~ zap_labels(leftrigh))) %>% 
  group_by(dolefidl) %>% 
  summarise(leftrigh = mean(leftrigh, na.rm = TRUE), 
            n = n())) 

# A tibble: 7 × 3
  dolefidl                   leftrigh     n
  <fct>                         <dbl> <int>
1 Agree strongly                 2.36   172
2 Agree                          2.50   612
3 Neither agree nor disagree     2.64  1170
4 Disagree                       2.55   967
5 Disagree strongly              2.07   265
6 Not answered                   2.13    72
7 <NA>                         NaN      730



dynamic_str_wrap <- function(text, plot_width = 7, num_labels = 1, char_width = 0.1, margin = 0.1) {
  max_label_width <- (plot_width * (1 - margin)) / num_labels
  max_chars <- max_label_width / char_width
  str_wrap(text, width = floor(max_chars))
}

wrap_titles <- function(title, plot_width = 7, char_width = 0.09) {
  max_chars <- floor(plot_width / char_width)
  wrapped_title <- str_wrap(title, width = max_chars)
  return(wrapped_title)
}

num_labels <- length(levels(fct_drop(leftrigh$dolefidl)))

x_lab <- var_label(bsa[[names(leftrigh[1])]])
x_lab_wrap <- wrap_titles(x_lab)

y_lab <- var_label(bsa[[names(leftrigh[2])]])
y_lab_wrap <- wrap_titles(y_lab, plot_width = 5)

title <- paste0(y_lab, " by ", x_lab)
title_wrap <- wrap_titles(title)

buffer <- mean(leftrigh$leftrigh, na.rm = TRUE) / 4
ylim <- c(0, ceiling(max(leftrigh$leftrigh, na.rm = TRUE) + buffer))

(plot <- leftrigh %>% 
  filter(!is.na(dolefidl)) %>%
  mutate(dolefidl = map_chr(as.character(dolefidl), dynamic_str_wrap, plot_width = 7, num_labels = num_labels, char_width = 0.08, margin = 0.1)) %>%
  mutate(dolefidl = factor(dolefidl, levels = unique(dolefidl))) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = dolefidl, y = leftrigh)) + 
  geom_col() + 
  geom_text(aes(label = round(leftrigh, 1)), vjust = -1.5, size = 11, size.unit = "pt") + 
  theme_gray() + 
  labs(title = title_wrap, 
       x = x_lab_wrap, 
       y = y_lab_wrap)  + 
  coord_cartesian(ylim = ylim) + 
  theme(
    plot.margin = margin(1,1,1,1),
    text = element_text(size = 11), # This seems to have no effect??? 
    plot.title = element_text(size = 13, face = "bold"),
    axis.title =  element_text(size = 11, face = "bold"), 
    axis.text.x = element_text(margin = margin(b = 10), size = 11), 
    axis.text.y = element_text(margin = margin(l = 10),  size = 11)
  )) 







# Retrospectivly add colour
plot + geom_col(fill = "#619CFF") # ggplot blue







plot + geom_col(fill = "#7082d4") # natcen blue 







plot + geom_col(aes(fill = dolefidl)) + 
  theme(legend.position = "none")







plot + 
  geom_col(aes(fill = dolefidl)) + 
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") + 
  theme(legend.position = "none")










5.7.2 Bar chart for 2 way crosstab - clustered

(leftrigh_sex <- bsa %>% 
  mutate(dolefidl = as_factor(dolefidl), 
         leftrigh = case_when(leftrigh == 9 ~ NA, 
                              TRUE ~ zap_labels(leftrigh)), 
         sex = as_factor(Rsex)) %>% 
  group_by(sex, dolefidl) %>% 
  summarise(leftrigh = mean(leftrigh, na.rm = TRUE), 
            n = n())) 

`summarise()` has grouped output by 'sex'. You can override using the `.groups`
argument.

freq <- function(dat, var) { 

x_var <- as.character(substitute(var))

table <- dat %>%
  filter(!is.na({{var}})) %>%
  group_by({{var}}) %>%
  summarise(n = n()) %>%
  mutate(p = 100 * (n / sum(n)))

table$x_label <- var_label(dat[[x_var]])

return(table)
  
}

dynamic_str_wrap <- function(text, plot_width = 7, num_labels = 1, char_width = 0.1, margin = 0.1) {
  max_label_width <- (plot_width * (1 - margin)) / num_labels
  max_chars <- max_label_width / char_width
  str_wrap(text, width = floor(max_chars))
}

wrap_titles <- function(title, plot_width = 7, char_width = 0.09) {
  max_chars <- floor(plot_width / char_width)
  wrapped_title <- str_wrap(title, width = max_chars)
  return(wrapped_title)
}

bar <- function(dat, colour = NULL, palette = NULL) { 

# Define some potential custom palettes
nbl <- nc_colours[,1] # extract blue
npi <- nc_colours[,2] 
nmg <- nc_colours[,3] 
npu <- nc_colours[,4] 
nor <- nc_colours[,5] 

nbl_oppo2 <- nc_colours[c(1,4),1] # extract blue
npi_oppo2 <- nc_colours[c(1,4),2] 
nmg_oppo2 <- nc_colours[c(1,4),3] 
npu_oppo2 <- nc_colours[c(1,4),4] 
nor_oppo2 <- nc_colours[c(1,4),5] 

nbl_seq3 <- nc_colours[c(1,3,5),1] # extract blue
npi_seq3 <- nc_colours[c(1,3,5),2] 
nmg_seq3 <- nc_colours[c(1,3,5),3] 
npu_seq3 <- nc_colours[c(1,3,5),4] 
nor_seq3 <- nc_colours[c(1,3,5),5] 

ncl_5to1 <- unlist(c(as.vector(nc_colours[1,]), as.vector(nc_colours[2,]), as.vector(nc_colours[3,]), as.vector(nc_colours[4,]), as.vector(nc_colours[5,]))) %>% unname
ncl_oppo2 <- nc_colours %>% slice(1,4) %>% pivot_longer(cols = everything()) %>% arrange(name) %>% select(-name) %>% as_vector() %>% unname
ncl_seq3 <- nc_colours %>% slice(1,3,5) %>% pivot_longer(cols = everything()) %>% arrange(name) %>% select(-name) %>% as_vector() %>% unname
    
dynamic_str_wrap <- function(text, plot_width = 7, num_labels = 1, char_width = 0.1, margin = 0.1) {
  max_label_width <- (plot_width * (1 - margin)) / num_labels
  max_chars <- max_label_width / char_width
  str_wrap(text, width = floor(max_chars))
}

wrap_titles <- function(title, plot_width = 7, char_width = 0.09) {
  max_chars <- floor(plot_width / char_width)
  wrapped_title <- str_wrap(title, width = max_chars)
  return(wrapped_title)
}

num_labels <- length(levels(fct_drop(hadvice[[1]])))

x_lab <- unique(dat[,4])
x_lab_wrap <- wrap_titles(x_lab)

# y_lab <- var_label(bsa[[names(hadvice[2])]])

title <- paste0(x_lab)
title_wrap <- wrap_titles(title)

buffer <- mean(dat$p, na.rm = TRUE) / 4
ylim <- c(0, ceiling(max(dat$p, na.rm = TRUE) + buffer))

x_var <- names(dat)[1]

(plot <- dat %>% 
  filter(!is.na(1)) %>%
  mutate(!!sym(x_var) := map_chr(as.character(!!sym(x_var)), dynamic_str_wrap, plot_width = 7, num_labels = num_labels, char_width = 0.08, margin = 0.1)) %>%
  mutate(!!sym(x_var) := factor(!!sym(x_var), levels = unique(!!sym(x_var)))) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = !!sym(x_var), y = p)) + 
  geom_col() + 
  geom_text(aes(label = round(p, 1)), vjust = -1.5, size = 11, size.unit = "pt") + 
  theme_gray() + 
  labs(title = title_wrap, 
       x = NULL, 
       y = "%"
       )  + 
  coord_cartesian(ylim = ylim) + 
  theme(
    plot.margin = margin(1,1,1,1),
    text = element_text(size = 11), # This seems to have no effect??? 
    plot.title = element_text(size = 13, face = "bold"),
    axis.title =  element_text(size = 11, face = "bold"), 
    axis.title.y =  element_text(angle = 0, vjust = 0.5), 
    axis.text.x = element_text(margin = margin(b = 10), size = 11), 
    axis.text.y = element_text(margin = margin(l = 10),  size = 11)
  )) 

if (!is_null(colour)) {
  
  if(colour == "pal") {
plot <- plot + 
  geom_col(aes(fill = !!sym(x_var))) + 
  theme(legend.position = "none")

 if(!is_null(palette)) {
   if (palette %in% rownames(RColorBrewer::brewer.pal.info)) {
     
plot <- plot + scale_fill_brewer(palette = palette)

   } else {

plot <- plot + scale_fill_manual(values = get(palette))
     
      }
    }
  } else {
    
plot <- plot + geom_col(fill = colour) 
  }
  
}

return(plot)
  
}

mean_func <- function(dat, x, y) {

  y_var <- as.character(substitute(y))

  table <- dat %>% 
  filter(!is.na({{x}})) %>% 
  mutate(!!sym(x_var) := fct_drop(as_factor({{x}}))) %>%
  group_by({{x}}) %>%
  summarise(!!sym(y_var) := mean({{y}}, na.rm = TRUE), n = n())

  table$x_label <- var_label(dat[[x_var]])
  table$y_label <- var_label(dat[[y_var]])

  return(table)
  
}

   dynamic_str_wrap <- function(text, plot_width = 7, num_labels = 1, char_width = 0.1, margin = 0.1) {
    max_label_width <- (plot_width * (1 - margin)) / num_labels
    max_chars <- max_label_width / char_width
    str_wrap(text, width = floor(max_chars))
  }

    wrapped_title <- str_wrap(title, width = max_chars)
    return(wrapped_title)
  }

       x = x_lab_wrap, 
       y = y_lab_wrap)  + 
  coord_cartesian(ylim = ylim) + 
  theme(
    plot.margin = margin(1,1,1,1),
    text = element_text(size = 11), # This seems to have no effect??? 
    plot.title = element_text(size = 13, face = "bold"),
    axis.title =  element_text(size = 11, face = "bold"), 
    axis.text.x = element_text(margin = margin(b = 10), size = 11), 
    axis.text.y = element_text(margin = margin(l = 10),  size = 11)
  )) 
  
  if (!is.null(.fill)) { 
    
  plot <- plot + geom_col(fill = .fill) # ggplot blue
    
  }
  
  if (!is.null(.pal_var)) { 
    
  plot <- plot + geom_col(aes(fill = !!sym(x_var))) + 
    theme(legend.position = "none")  
  
    if(is.character(.pal_var)) { 
   
           if (.pal_var %in% rownames(RColorBrewer::brewer.pal.info)) {

plot <- plot + scale_fill_manual(values = get(.pal_var))
     
      }
   
    }
      
    }
  
  return(list(num_labels, x_lab_wrap, y_lab_wrap, title_wrap, buffer, ylim, plot))
  
}

title: 6  Bar chart functions
text:
6.1 Philosophy
To arrive at a simple, usable piece of code, that is useful, as early as possible.
To write generic functions - not too bespoke to a given use-case.
Here, I initially wrote something tightly linked to the output format of calc_stats. It depended on column names and content more than it should have. I have now adjusted to make bar() more generic, taking any factor column as it’s x-axis and any numeric column as the y-axis, with labels simply fed as strings.
This can then be linked quite simply to the output of calc_stats, but is also useful on its own elsewhere.

title: 6  Bar chart functions
text:
6.1.1 Purpose
Take a dataframe with one categorical (ideally factor) variable to use as the x-axis and a second numeric variable to use as the y-axis and presents this nicely in a ggplot. Add variable labels as x- and y- axis titles, and allow a user defined plot title.
These plot values in a dataframe, they don’t calculate new statistics from a larger dataframe.

title: 6  Bar chart function
text:
6.2 A function

6.2.1 Trial 1

# wrap_text() -----------------------------------------------------------------
# Wraps a string to fit within a ggplot plot area.
# plot_width: estimated plot width in inches
# num_labels: number of bars sharing the axis — defaults to 1, which makes
#             this suitable for titles and axis labels as well as tick labels
# char_width: approximate width of one character in inches — adjust for font size
# margin:     proportion of plot width to treat as unusable

wrap_text <- function(text, plot_width = 7, num_labels = 1, char_width = 0.09, margin = 0.1) {
  usable_width <- plot_width * (1 - margin)
  max_chars    <- floor((usable_width / num_labels) / char_width)
  str_wrap(text, width = max_chars)
}


# bar() -----------------------------------------------------------------------
# Bar chart for a single categorical x numeric variable.
# x:           unquoted name of the categorical column (ideally a factor) for the x-axis
# y:           unquoted name of the numeric column for the y-axis
# x_lab:       optional character string for the x-axis title
# y_lab:       optional character string for the y-axis title — if longer than 3
#              characters the label is rotated vertical and wrapped to plot height
# title:       optional — left empty if not supplied
# fill:        bar colour — "blue" (default), "softblue", "green", "orange"
# plot_width:  estimated plot width in inches — used for label wrapping, default 7
# plot_height: estimated plot height in inches — used for y-axis label wrapping
#              when the label is long, default 4

bar <- function(dat, x, y, x_lab = NULL, y_lab = NULL, title = NULL,
                fill = "blue", plot_width = 7, plot_height = 4) {

  fill_options <- c(
    blue     = "#2E75B6",
    softblue = "#5B9BD5",
    green    = "#70AD47",
    orange   = "#ED7D31"
  )

  fill <- match.arg(fill, names(fill_options))
  fill_colour <- fill_options[[fill]]

  x_var <- as.character(substitute(x))
  y_var <- as.character(substitute(y))

  x_lab_wrap <- if (!is.null(x_lab)) wrap_text(x_lab, plot_width = plot_width) else NULL
  title_wrap <- if (!is.null(title)) wrap_text(title, plot_width = plot_width) else NULL

  # y-axis label — horizontal for short labels, vertical and wrapped for long
  if (!is.null(y_lab) && nchar(y_lab) > 3) {
    y_lab_final  <- wrap_text(y_lab, plot_width = plot_height)
    y_axis_theme <- element_text(angle = 90, hjust = 0.5, size = 11, face = "bold")
  } else if (!is.null(y_lab)) {
    y_lab_final  <- y_lab
    y_axis_theme <- element_text(angle = 0, vjust = 0.5, size = 11, face = "bold")
  } else {
    y_lab_final  <- NULL
    y_axis_theme <- element_text(angle = 0, vjust = 0.5, size = 11, face = "bold")
  }

  num_labels <- length(levels(fct_drop(as.factor(dat[[x_var]]))))

  buffer <- mean(dat[[y_var]], na.rm = TRUE) / 4
  ylim   <- c(0, ceiling(max(dat[[y_var]], na.rm = TRUE) + buffer))
  
  bar_width <- case_when(
  num_labels <= 3 ~ 0.4,
  num_labels <= 5 ~ 0.6,
  num_labels <= 8 ~ 0.75,
  TRUE            ~ 0.9
  )

  dat %>%
    filter(!is.na(!!sym(x_var))) %>%
    mutate(
      !!sym(x_var) := map_chr(
        as.character(!!sym(x_var)),
        wrap_text,
        num_labels = num_labels,
        char_width = 0.08
      ),
      !!sym(x_var) := factor(!!sym(x_var), levels = unique(!!sym(x_var)))
    ) %>%
    ggplot(aes(x = !!sym(x_var), y = !!sym(y_var))) +
    geom_col(fill = fill_colour, width = bar_width) +
    geom_text(aes(label = round(!!sym(y_var), 1)), vjust = -1.5, size = 11, size.unit = "pt") +
    theme_linedraw() +
    labs(
      title = title_wrap,
      x     = x_lab_wrap,
      y     = y_lab_final
    ) +
    coord_cartesian(ylim = ylim) +
    theme(
      plot.margin  = margin(1, 1, 1, 1),
      plot.title   = element_text(size = 13, face = "bold"),
      axis.title   = element_text(size = 11, face = "bold"),
      axis.title.y = y_axis_theme,
      axis.text.x  = element_text(margin = margin(b = 10), size = 11),
      axis.text.y  = element_text(margin = margin(l = 10), size = 11)
    )

}



6.2.2 Trial 2

# wrap_text() -----------------------------------------------------------------
# Wraps a string to fit within a ggplot plot area.
# plot_width: estimated plot width in inches
# num_labels: number of bars sharing the axis — defaults to 1, which makes
#             this suitable for titles and axis labels as well as tick labels
# char_width: approximate width of one character in inches — adjust for font size
# margin:     proportion of plot width to treat as unusable

wrap_text <- function(text, plot_width = 7, num_labels = 1, char_width = 0.09, margin = 0.1) {
  usable_width <- plot_width * (1 - margin)
  max_chars    <- floor((usable_width / num_labels) / char_width)
  str_wrap(text, width = max_chars)
}


# resolve_y_lab() -------------------------------------------------------------
# Resolves y-axis label text and theme element based on label length.
# Returns a named list: y_lab_final and y_axis_theme.
# Short labels (<=3 chars) are horizontal, longer labels are vertical and wrapped.

resolve_y_lab <- function(y_lab, plot_height = 4) {
  if (!is.null(y_lab) && nchar(y_lab) > 3) {
    list(
      y_lab_final  = wrap_text(y_lab, plot_width = plot_height),
      y_axis_theme = element_text(angle = 90, hjust = 0.5, size = 11, face = "bold")
    )
  } else if (!is.null(y_lab)) {
    list(
      y_lab_final  = y_lab,
      y_axis_theme = element_text(angle = 0, vjust = 0.5, size = 11, face = "bold")
    )
  } else {
    list(
      y_lab_final  = NULL,
      y_axis_theme = element_text(angle = 0, vjust = 0.5, size = 11, face = "bold")
    )
  }
}


# bar_width_from_n() ----------------------------------------------------------
# Scales bar width to the number of x-axis labels.
# Fewer bars get narrower widths to avoid them looking too fat.

bar_width_from_n <- function(num_labels) {
  case_when(
    num_labels <= 3 ~ 0.4,
    num_labels <= 5 ~ 0.6,
    num_labels <= 8 ~ 0.75,
    TRUE            ~ 0.9
  )
}


# bar() -----------------------------------------------------------------------
# Bar chart for a single categorical x numeric variable.
# x:           unquoted name of the categorical column (ideally a factor) for the x-axis
# y:           unquoted name of the numeric column for the y-axis
# x_lab:       optional character string for the x-axis title
# y_lab:       optional character string for the y-axis title — if longer than 3
#              characters the label is rotated vertical and wrapped to plot height
# title:       optional — left empty if not supplied
# fill:        bar colour — "blue" (default), "softblue", "green", "orange"
# plot_width:  estimated plot width in inches — used for label wrapping, default 7
# plot_height: estimated plot height in inches — used for y-axis label wrapping
#              when the label is long, default 4

bar <- function(dat, x, y, x_lab = NULL, y_lab = NULL, title = NULL,
                fill = "blue", plot_width = 7, plot_height = 4) {

  fill_options <- c(
    blue     = "#2E75B6",
    softblue = "#5B9BD5",
    green    = "#70AD47",
    orange   = "#ED7D31"
  )

  fill <- match.arg(fill, names(fill_options))
  fill_colour <- fill_options[[fill]]

  x_var <- as.character(substitute(x))
  y_var <- as.character(substitute(y))

  x_lab_wrap <- if (!is.null(x_lab)) wrap_text(x_lab, plot_width = plot_width) else NULL
  title_wrap <- if (!is.null(title)) wrap_text(title, plot_width = plot_width) else NULL

  y_lab_resolved <- resolve_y_lab(y_lab, plot_height)
  y_lab_final    <- y_lab_resolved$y_lab_final
  y_axis_theme   <- y_lab_resolved$y_axis_theme

  num_labels <- length(levels(fct_drop(as.factor(dat[[x_var]]))))
  bar_width  <- bar_width_from_n(num_labels)

  buffer <- mean(dat[[y_var]], na.rm = TRUE) / 4
  ylim   <- c(0, ceiling(max(dat[[y_var]], na.rm = TRUE) + buffer))

  dat %>%
    filter(!is.na(!!sym(x_var))) %>%
    mutate(
      !!sym(x_var) := map_chr(
        as.character(!!sym(x_var)),
        wrap_text,
        num_labels = num_labels,
        char_width = 0.08
      ),
      !!sym(x_var) := factor(!!sym(x_var), levels = unique(!!sym(x_var)))
    ) %>%
    ggplot(aes(x = !!sym(x_var), y = !!sym(y_var))) +
    geom_col(fill = fill_colour, width = bar_width) +
    geom_text(aes(label = round(!!sym(y_var), 1)), vjust = -1.5, size = 11, size.unit = "pt") +
    theme_linedraw() +
    labs(
      title = title_wrap,
      x     = x_lab_wrap,
      y     = y_lab_final
    ) +
    coord_cartesian(ylim = ylim) +
    theme(
      plot.margin  = margin(1, 1, 1, 1),
      plot.title   = element_text(size = 13, face = "bold"),
      axis.title   = element_text(size = 11, face = "bold"),
      axis.title.y = y_axis_theme,
      axis.text.x  = element_text(margin = margin(b = 10), size = 11),
      axis.text.y  = element_text(margin = margin(l = 10), size = 11)
    )

}


# bar_clustered() -------------------------------------------------------------
# Clustered bar chart for a categorical x numeric variable, grouped by a third
# factor variable displayed as fill colour.
# x:           unquoted name of the categorical column for the x-axis
# y:           unquoted name of the numeric column for the y-axis
# fill:        unquoted name of the grouping variable mapped to fill colour
# x_lab:       optional character string for the x-axis title
# y_lab:       optional character string for the y-axis title
# fill_lab:    optional character string for the legend title
# title:       optional — left empty if not supplied
# palette:     RColorBrewer palette name for fill colours, default "Set2"
# plot_width:  estimated plot width in inches — used for label wrapping, default 7
# plot_height: estimated plot height in inches — used for y-axis label wrapping
#              when the label is long, default 4

bar_clustered <- function(dat, x, y, fill, x_lab = NULL, y_lab = NULL,
                          fill_lab = NULL, title = NULL,
                          palette = "Set2", plot_width = 7, plot_height = 4) {

  x_var    <- as.character(substitute(x))
  y_var    <- as.character(substitute(y))
  fill_var <- as.character(substitute(fill))

  x_lab_wrap <- if (!is.null(x_lab)) wrap_text(x_lab, plot_width = plot_width) else NULL
  title_wrap <- if (!is.null(title)) wrap_text(title, plot_width = plot_width) else NULL

  y_lab_resolved <- resolve_y_lab(y_lab, plot_height)
  y_lab_final    <- y_lab_resolved$y_lab_final
  y_axis_theme   <- y_lab_resolved$y_axis_theme

  num_labels <- length(levels(fct_drop(as.factor(dat[[x_var]]))))

  buffer <- mean(dat[[y_var]], na.rm = TRUE) / 4
  ylim   <- c(0, ceiling(max(dat[[y_var]], na.rm = TRUE) + buffer))

  dat %>%
    filter(!is.na(!!sym(x_var))) %>%
    mutate(
      !!sym(x_var) := map_chr(
        as.character(!!sym(x_var)),
        wrap_text,
        num_labels = num_labels,
        char_width = 0.08
      ),
      !!sym(x_var) := factor(!!sym(x_var), levels = unique(!!sym(x_var))),
      !!sym(fill_var) := as.factor(!!sym(fill_var))
    ) %>%
    ggplot(aes(x = !!sym(x_var), y = !!sym(y_var), fill = !!sym(fill_var))) +
    geom_col(position = "dodge2") +
    geom_text(
      aes(label = round(!!sym(y_var), 1)),
      position = position_dodge2(0.9),
      vjust = -1.5,
      size = 11,
      size.unit = "pt"
    ) +
    scale_fill_brewer(palette = palette) +
    theme_linedraw() +
    labs(
      title = title_wrap,
      x     = x_lab_wrap,
      y     = y_lab_final,
      fill  = fill_lab
    ) +
    coord_cartesian(ylim = ylim) +
    theme(
      plot.margin  = margin(1, 1, 1, 1),
      plot.title   = element_text(size = 13, face = "bold"),
      axis.title   = element_text(size = 11, face = "bold"),
      axis.title.y = y_axis_theme,
      axis.text.x  = element_text(margin = margin(b = 10), size = 11),
      axis.text.y  = element_text(margin = margin(l = 10), size = 11),
      legend.title = element_text(size = 11, face = "bold"),
      legend.position = "bottom"
    )

}
